THE GATEZero to N2 — kana, kanji, grammar, drills. 50 chapters.
Progress0 / 50

The Gate

Japanese built brick by brick — from your first particle to N2 reading. Vocabulary is the spine: every chapter carries a word set, and the Dojo drills everything you've finished, every day. Kanji readings are hidden until you hover — try to read first, then peek.

JP-00

The Dojo — your daily ten minutes

Language is the one subject where showing up daily beats showing up brilliantly. This chapter is not read once — it is visited every day, before anything else.

This book has one job: get you reading real Japanese at N3, then N2. The route is fixed — grammar in small bricks, vocabulary in themed sets, and retrieval practice every single day. Reading a word does almost nothing for memory. Being forced to recall it — meaning from kanji, reading from kanji, word from meaning — is what moves it to long-term storage. That is what the Dojo below does: it pulls vocabulary from every chapter you have marked done and fires twenty mixed questions at you.

The daily loop

  1. Open the Dojo and run one drill session (20 questions, ~5 minutes). It only quizzes chapters you've finished, so it is always fair — and it gets harder as you progress, because the pool grows.
  2. Read today's chapter (or re-read one you feel shaky on — re-reading a shaky chapter is progress, not failure).
  3. Run that chapter's own drills until you score at least 8/10 on each mode.
  4. Mark it done. The streak counter in the sidebar is watching. Streaks start at two.
How fast is N2 from zero?

Honest numbers: N5+N4 is roughly 300 hours of study, N3 another ~350, N2 another ~600 on top. At 30 focused minutes a day plus immersion on weekends, N3 is a realistic 12–18 month target and N2 a 2–3 year target. This book is the grammar-and-core-vocab layer of that journey; from Part IV onward it tells you exactly what immersion to add. There is no shortcut, but there is a straight line — this is it.

Rules of the Dojo

  • Answer before you reveal. Commit — say it out loud or write it. Recognition feels like knowledge; recall is knowledge.
  • Wrong answers are the product. Each miss tells you exactly which chapter to revisit. The drill names the chapter on every card.
  • Furigana discipline. Readings everywhere in this book are hidden until you hover or tap the word. Always attempt the reading first. You did the hard work of learning kanji — this is where it starts paying interest.
  • Use your ears. Most words and every example sentence have a 🔊 button (your browser's built-in Japanese voice — no network needed). Hearing the word as you recall it doubles the encoding.

What "done" means in this book

Mark a chapter done when: you've read it, you can pass its drills at 8/10, and you could explain its core grammar point to someone else in two sentences. Not when you've merely scrolled to the bottom. The progress bar is a contract with yourself — keep it honest and it will carry you to the gate.

KA-01

Hiragana I — your first 25 letters

Japanese is spelled with 46 phonetic letters called hiragana. Learn to read them and every word in this book opens up. This chapter and the next take most people 3–6 days total — the single best investment in your entire journey.

How the system works

Every hiragana is one syllable with a fixed sound that never changes. No "silent e", no "gh" nonsense — か is ka, today, tomorrow, forever. The whole set is a 5-vowel grid: every consonant row uses the same five vowels in the same order: a・i・u・e・o.

  • あ a — as in "father". い i — as in "machine". う u — as in "flu" (lips relaxed, not rounded). え e — as in "egg". お o — as in "or" (short).
  • Say the vowel row out loud right now: あ・い・う・え・お — a, i, u, e, o. That melody repeats through the entire script.
How to study a row (Tofugu-style)

Look at the shape → hear it (click it in the chart below) → say it out loud → move on after ~10 seconds. Don't aim for perfection on pass one; aim for five fast passes. Recognition beats recall at this stage, and the quiz below builds recall for you. Handwriting is optional — reading is the skill this book needs.

Rows 1–5: あ・か・さ・た・な

RowaiueoWatch out
—あ aい iう uえ eお oお vs あ look similar at first
Kか kaき kiく kuけ keこ koき has a little hat
Sさ saし shiす suせ seそ soし is shi, not "si"
Tた taち chiつ tsuて teと toち = chi, つ = tsu
Nな naに niぬ nuね neの noぬ/め and ね/れ/わ are the classic mix-ups

Three irregulars in the whole set so far — し shi, ち chi, つ tsu — and they're the ones English speakers pronounce correctly by accident anyway.

Hear them, then drill them

Click any cell to hear it. Then run the quiz until you score 12/15 twice in a row — that's your "done" bar for this chapter.

Your first real words

You can already read these — no kanji, no tricks. Sound each one out:

あさasa — morning
ねこneko — cat
すしsushi — you knew this one
たかいtakai — expensive
KA-02

Hiragana II — the rest, plus the sound marks

Twenty-one more letters, two little marks that create 25 extra sounds for free, and the three rules (small っ, ん, long vowels) that make written Japanese match spoken Japanese.

Rows 6–10: は・ま・や・ら・わ

RowaiueoWatch out
Hは haひ hiふ fuへ heほ hoふ is soft, between f and h
Mま maみ miむ muめ meも moめ vs ぬ — め has no loop-tail
Yや ya—ゆ yu—よ yoonly three — the gaps are historical
Rら raり riる ruれ reろ roJapanese r = tap the roof of your mouth, between r/l/d
Wわ wa———を oを is only used as a grammar particle
—ん nthe only stand-alone consonant; a full beat of its own

Dakuten ゛and handakuten ゜— 25 sounds for free

Two tiny marks in the top-right corner voice a consonant. You don't learn new shapes — you learn one rule:

か→がk → g (ga)さ→ざs → z (za) — し→じ is ji
た→だt → d (da)は→ばh → b (ba)
は→ぱh → p (pa) — the little circle ゜く→ぐevery row member follows: き→ぎ, け→げ…

Combination sounds: きゃ・きゅ・きょ

An い-column letter plus a small や/ゆ/よ blends into one beat: きゃ kya, しゅ shu, ちょ cho, じゃ ja. Size matters: きや (big や) is ki-ya, two beats; きゃ is kya, one beat.

The three timing rules

  • Small っ = a one-beat pause that doubles the next consonant: きって ki-t-te (stamp). Hold your mouth for a beat.
  • ん is its own beat: こんにちは = ko-n-ni-chi-wa, five beats.
  • Long vowels are written out: おかあさん okaasan (mother), せんせい sensee-ish (the えい combo sounds like a long e), とうきょう tookyoo (both おう combos are long o).
The two spelling quirks you must know

As grammar particles, は is pronounced wa and へ is pronounced e. こんにちは ends in wa for exactly this reason — it's an old particle fossilized in the greeting. The romaji lines in this book already apply the rule for you.

Drill it all

KA-03

Katakana I — same sounds, sharper clothes

Katakana writes exactly the same 46 sounds as hiragana with different shapes, and Japan uses it for foreign words — which means you already know a huge chunk of katakana vocabulary. コーヒー is just "coffee".

Why bother? Because menus.

Restaurant menus, tech vocabulary, store signs, your own name — katakana is everywhere in modern Japan. Beginners consistently under-study it and then stall reading real text. Give it the same 3–5 days you gave hiragana; the payoff arrives immediately because most katakana words are English in disguise.

The grid, first half

RowaiueoHiragana twin
—ア aイ iウ uエ eオ oあいうえお
Kカ kaキ kiク kuケ keコ koかきくけこ — カ is angular か
Sサ saシ shiス suセ seソ soシ and ソ — see the warning box
Tタ taチ chiツ tsuテ teト toツ vs シ — same warning box
Nナ naニ niヌ nuネ neノ noニ really is just に's right half
The four demons: シ ツ ソ ン

shi・tsu・so・n — the most-confused shapes in the whole writing system. The trick is stroke direction: in シ (shi) and ン (n) the long stroke sweeps up from bottom-left; in ツ (tsu) and ソ (so) it sweeps down from top-right. Also シ's two dots lie flat (like し's smile), ツ's stand upright. The quiz below hammers exactly these four.

Read the menu already

コーヒーkoohii — coffee
アイスクリームaisukuriimu — ice cream
タクシーtakushii — taxi
カメラkamera — camera

Drill

KA-04

Katakana II — the rest, ー, and loanword decoding

Finish the grid, meet the long-vowel dash, and learn the five conversion rules that turn katakana back into the English it came from.

The grid, second half

Rowaiueo
Hハ haヒ hiフ fuヘ heホ ho
Mマ maミ miム muメ meモ mo
Yヤ ya—ユ yu—ヨ yo
Rラ raリ riル ruレ reロ ro
W/Nワ waヲ o (rare)ン ndakuten work identically: ガ ザ ダ バ パ…

Decoding loanwords: the five rules

  • ー stretches the vowel: カー kaa (car), ビール biiru (beer).
  • English L becomes R-row: ホテル hoteru (hotel), サラダ sarada (salad).
  • Consonant clusters get vowels inserted (usually u, after t/d it's o): ストレス sutoresu (stress), ベッド beddo (bed).
  • V often becomes B: テレビ terebi (TV). TH becomes S/Z: サンキュー.
  • New combo sounds for foreign noises: ファ fa, ティ ti, ウェ we, ディ di — small vowels glued on: パーティー paatii (party).
Try it — no dictionary allowed

Decode these with the rules: レストラン・コンビニ・エレベーター・バスケットボール・クレジットカード. (restaurant, convenience store, elevator, basketball, credit card.) Feel that? That's ~500 free vocabulary words you just unlocked.

Drill — grid, demons, and real loanwords

KA-05

Typing Japanese — set up your keyboard today

You type romaji, the computer makes kana, and a spacebar press turns kana into kanji. Ten minutes of setup, and it quietly reinforces every kana you just learned.

Setup on Windows (2 minutes)

  1. Settings → Time & language → Language & region → Add a language → 日本語 (Japanese) → Install. (You can untick "set as display language".)
  2. Press Win + Space to switch between English and Japanese keyboards any time.
  3. In Japanese mode, type konnichiha → こんにちは appears. Press Space to convert to kanji candidates, Enter to accept.
While you're in that Settings screen…

Click 日本語 → Language options → download the Speech feature too. That installs the Japanese text-to-speech voice this book's 🔊 buttons use. Two birds, one visit.

The typing rules that aren't obvious

んtype nn (or n before a consonant): sennsei? No — sensei works; hon needs honn
っ (small tsu)double the consonant: kitte → きって
をtype wo
づ／ぢtype du / di
Small vowels (ティ etc.)thi for ティ, or x/l prefix for any small kana: xtu → っ
ー (long dash)the hyphen key, in Japanese mode

Why this matters for the N5 plan

Typing is active recall of kana disguised as a chore — every word you type re-tests you. From this point on, when a drill shows you a word, occasionally type it into a notes file. Also: the JLPT is paper-based, but every dictionary, Anki deck, and website you'll use from now on assumes you can type. Do the setup today; it compounds.

Checkpoint — end of the 入門 part

You can read both kana sets and type them. That was the steepest week of the whole journey. Everything from here is vocabulary, patterns, and reps — mark this done and walk through the gate to the kanji.

KJ-01

Kanji I — numbers, money, calendar

The N5 exam tests roughly 106 kanji. This part teaches all of them in five themed sets, starting with the 22 you'll see on every price tag, clock, and calendar in Japan.

How to learn a kanji (the honest method)

A kanji is not a letter — it's a meaning with (usually) two readings: the on'yomi (used in kanji compounds: 三月 san-gatsu) and the kun'yomi (used alone or with hiragana tails: 三つ mit-tsu). The working method, borrowed from the WaniKani/mnemonic school:

  1. Look at the shape and find a picture in it. 一二三 are literally one, two, three strokes. 川 is a river's three streams. 山 is a mountain with three peaks. Sillier stories stick better — make your own.
  2. Learn the readings through words, never as a list. Each kanji card below carries its most useful N5 words — drill those and the readings install themselves.
  3. Trust recognition. The JLPT never asks you to write a kanji, only to read it. Recognition + reading is the whole game at this level.
Pace

Five to seven kanji a day is a sustainable burn. Each of these five chapters is a 3–4 day block: read the cards once, then run both drills daily until you hold 8/10. The Dojo keeps testing you afterward, so nothing rusts.

The cards — 数字とカレンダー

Where you'll meet them today

三百円さんびゃくえんです。That's 300 yen.
今日きょうは四月十日しがつとおかです。Today is April 10th. (今日 is an irregular fused reading — memorize it as one word)
KJ-02

Kanji II — weekdays, time, people

The five elements that name the days of the week, the words that slice up a day, and the small cast of humans every N5 sentence talks about.

The weekday system

Japanese weekdays are element + 曜日 (yōbi): 月曜日 Moon-day (Monday), 火曜日 Fire-day (Tuesday), 水曜日 Water-day, 木曜日 Tree-day, 金曜日 Gold-day, 土曜日 Earth-day, 日曜日 Sun-day. Poetic, systematic, and worth the price of five element kanji — which also happen to mean fire, water, wood, gold/money, and earth in hundreds of other words.

Picture hooks

火 is a person waving flames. 水 is a splash frozen mid-air. 木 is a tree with drooping branches; two trees 林 are woods, three 森 a forest (that's an N4 bonus, but the logic is the point). 金 is treasure under a roof. 土 is a plant sprouting from the ground.

The cards — 曜日と時間と人

In the wild

金曜日きんようびの午後ごご、友達ともだちに会あいます。Friday afternoon, I'm meeting a friend.
先生せんせいは女おんなの人ひとです。The teacher is a woman.
KJ-03

Kanji III — school, language, doing things

The study-and-action set: the kanji of learning itself, plus the core verbs — go, come, eat, drink, see, hear, read, write, buy. These carry more N5 sentences than any other group.

Verb kanji work differently

Most kanji in this set live inside verbs, where the kanji carries the meaning-root and hiragana carries the conjugation: 食たべる・食たべます・食たべて — the 食 never changes, the tail does. So when you drill these cards, you're really drilling the verbs from JP-04 in their true written form. This is where your kana knowledge and kanji knowledge fuse into actual reading.

Picture hooks

学 is a child 子 under a roof collecting knowledge. 見 is an eye 目 on legs, walking around looking. 聞 is an ear 耳 pressed to a gate 門. 食 is a roof over a pot of rice. 買 is a net over shells (shells were money). 行 is a crossroads. 出 is a foot stepping out of a container; 入 is the same doorway entered.

The cards — 学びと動き

In the wild

日本語にほんごの本ほんを読よみます。I read a Japanese book. (三つの漢字 — all from this part)
学校がっこうで何なにを買かいましたか。What did you buy at school?
KJ-04

Kanji IV — nature, places, directions

Mountains and rivers, weather, vehicles, and the six direction words — the kanji of every train station sign and every address in the country.

These are the most pictographic of all

This set is where kanji's picture-origin is most visible: 山 is a mountain ridge, 川 flowing water, 田 a rice paddy seen from above, 雨 rain drops under a cloud-roof, 車 a cart seen from above (axle and wheels). Direction pairs come as sets: 上/下 above/below (a mark above vs below a line), 右/左 right/left, 東西南北 the four compass points — 東 (east) is the sun 日 rising behind a tree 木.

Station-sign survival

Four kanji pairs cover most signage: 入口 entrance / 出口 exit, 東口・西口・南口・北口 east/west/south/north exit, 上り・下り inbound/outbound trains. If you learn nothing else for your first trip to Japan, learn these.

The cards — 自然と場所

In the wild

駅えきの東口ひがしぐちで会あいましょう。Let's meet at the station's east exit.
山やまの上うえから川かわが見みえます。You can see the river from the top of the mountain.
KJ-05

Kanji V — body, adjectives, and the finish line

The last N5 set: body parts, animals, and the adjective kanji — plus a handful of high-frequency N4 kanji thrown in early because you'll meet them constantly anyway.

Adjective kanji: the stems of JP-05

Just like verb kanji, adjective kanji are stems with hiragana tails: 高たかい・高たかくない・高たかかった. Drill this set and the adjective chapter's vocabulary snaps into its written form. The body-part set (手足目口耳) pays double: they're all N5 words on their own AND the building blocks of hundreds of later kanji (remember 聞 = ear at the gate).

About the last few cards

The final cards (新・古・多・少・早・近・遠・強・弱) are technically N4 — but they're the natural pairs of N5 words you already drill (new/old, many/few, strong/weak), they appear constantly in N5 reading practice, and learning opposites as pairs is cheaper than learning them apart. Consider them a head start, not homework.

The cards — 体と形容詞

Checkpoint — the kanji gate

That's the full N5 set plus a head start. From here, every chapter's vocabulary tables become readable the honest way: kanji first, hover only when stuck, romaji off as soon as you dare. When all five KJ chapters hold at 8/10 in both drill modes, the 文字・語彙 section of the exam is substantially yours.

私わたしは毎日まいにち新あたらしい漢字かんじを五いつつ学まなびます。I learn five new kanji every day. (You can read this sentence now. All of it.)
JP-01

How Japanese works — sounds, scripts, sentence shape

Before the first grammar rule, get the three load-bearing facts straight: Japanese is built from mora, written in three scripts with three different jobs, and glued together by particles — not word order.

The sound system: mora, not syllables

Japanese rhythm is counted in mora — equal beats. Each kana is one beat. とうきょう (Tokyo) is not two syllables; it is four beats: と・う・きょ・う. This matters because three things that trip up learners are all "extra beats":

  • Long vowels are two beats. おばさんobasan (aunt) vs おばあさん (grandmother). One extra あ, completely different relative. Same with ビル (building) vs ビール (beer).
  • The small っ is a silent beat — a one-beat pause that doubles the next consonant. きて (come!) vs きって (stamp). Your mouth holds position for one beat.
  • ん is a full beat of its own. こんにちは is ko・n・ni・chi・wa — five beats.
Mental model

Japanese is a metronome. Every kana (plus っ and ん and each half of a long vowel) gets one tick. If you speak with even ticks, you sound Japanese even with a bad accent; if you compress beats the way English does, you become hard to understand even with perfect grammar.

Three scripts, three jobs

Hiragana ひらがな

The grammatical skeleton: particles, verb endings, native words without common kanji. Curvy, everywhere, non-negotiable. If a word is grammar, it's hiragana.

Katakana カタカナ

The foreign-word script: loanwords (コーヒー coffee), foreign names, onomatopoeia, emphasis (like italics). Angular. Same sounds as hiragana, different shapes.

Kanji 漢字

The content: noun roots, verb stems, adjective stems. 食たべる — kanji carries the meaning "eat", hiragana carries the conjugation. You know these already; this book makes you read them.

A real sentence braids all three: 私わたしはコーヒーを飲のみます — kanji roots (私, 飲), katakana loanword (コーヒー), hiragana grammar (は, を, みます). Once you see the braid, you can skim a sentence and instantly know where the meaning lives (kanji) and where the grammar lives (hiragana tails).

Readings: why 日 has six pronunciations

Most kanji have at least two readings: the on'yomi (Chinese-derived, used in kanji compounds) and kun'yomi (native, used when the kanji stands alone or with hiragana tails). The rule of thumb that is right ~80% of the time:

  • Kanji + kanji → on'yomi. 学生がくせい, 電車でんしゃ, 日本にほん.
  • Kanji alone or + hiragana → kun'yomi. 水みず, 食たべる, 高たかい.

Don't memorize reading lists in the abstract — learn readings through vocabulary, which is exactly what every chapter's word set does. After a few hundred words the patterns install themselves and you start guessing new readings correctly.

Why the mess exists (2-minute history)

Japan imported Chinese characters around the 5th century for a language totally unrelated to Chinese. Each character arrived with its Chinese-ish pronunciation (the on'yomi — often several, from different centuries and regions of China), and was also glued onto existing native Japanese words (the kun'yomi). So 水 is sui in compounds (水曜日・すいようび, Wednesday) but mizu alone. Annoying, but systematic — and the system becomes intuitive through volume, not analysis.

Sentence shape: the verb anchors the end, particles do the work

English is Subject-Verb-Object and meaning depends on order: "the dog bit the man" ≠ "the man bit the dog". Japanese is verb-final, and everything before the verb is tagged with a particle — a one-syllable label that announces the word's role. Because the labels carry the roles, the order of the labeled blocks barely matters:

犬いぬが男おとこを噛かんだ。The dog bit the man. (犬 tagged が = doer, 男 tagged を = target)
男おとこを犬いぬが噛かんだ。Same meaning, reordered — the particles kept the roles.
Mental model

A Japanese sentence is a stack of labeled boxes delivered to a verb. が = "doer", を = "direct object", に = "destination/target", で = "location of action/tool". The verb at the end opens the boxes. When you read, your eyes should jump to the particle after each chunk — the particle, not the position, tells you what you're holding.

Two more structural habits to install on day one: Japanese drops anything obvious from context (subjects, objects — a full sentence can be one verb: 食たべた？ "did (you) eat?"), and it has no articles, no plurals, no future tense — 本ほん is "a book / the book / books", and the non-past tense covers both "I eat" and "I will eat". Context, which the language trusts completely, disambiguates.

The kana, with your ears

You likely know the kana already — the instrument below is for two things: filling gaps fast (click any cell to hear it), and drilling the dakuten/combination rows that slow readers down (が・ぱ・きゃ lines). Make sure katakana is as automatic as hiragana; N-level texts will not wait for you to decode レストラン.

This chapter's vocabulary — the survival forty

Forty concrete nouns you will meet in every chapter from here on. These are deliberately kanji-first: hover for the reading, then drill until reading-recall is automatic. This is the routine for every chapter in the book.

JP-02

The first sentence — です, は, and questions

One pattern — X は Y です — gives you introductions, descriptions, opinions, and questions. Master its four forms and the two words that break English brains: は and が.

X は Y です — the equation sentence

The workhorse of early Japanese: "as for X, it is Y."

私わたしは学生がくせいです。I am a student.lit. "As for me — student — is."
これは水みずです。This is water.

は is the topic particle — written は but pronounced wa (a historical spelling, like English "knight"). It means "regarding X / as for X". です is the polite copula — "is/am/are". It conjugates through exactly four cells, and this 2×2 grid is the template every predicate in Japanese follows:

Non-pastPast
Affirmativeです
is		でした
was
Negativeじゃないです
is not		じゃなかったです
was not

じゃ is the spoken contraction of では; in writing and formal speech you'll see ではありません / ではありませんでした — same cells, stiffer register.

田中たなかさんは先生せんせいじゃないです。Tanaka-san is not a teacher.

Questions: add か, change nothing else

No inversion, no auxiliary "do" — append か and you have a question. Question words (何・なに/なん what, 誰・だれ who, どこ where) sit exactly where their answer would sit:

これは何なんですか。What is this?
トイレはどこですか。Where is the toilet? (the most load-bearing sentence in the language)
あの人ひとは誰だれですか。Who is that person?

この・その・あの — the three-way pointing system

English splits space two ways (this/that); Japanese splits it three ways, by whose territory the thing is in:

Near meNear youAway from bothQuestion
これ (this thing)それ (that thing)あれ (that thing over there)どれ (which thing)
この＋nounその＋nounあの＋nounどの＋noun
ここ (here)そこ (there)あそこ (over there)どこ (where)
The classic slip

これ／それ／あれ stand alone (これは本ほんです). この／その／あの must be glued to a noun (この本ほんは…). ×このは本です is the #1 beginner error. If there's no noun after it, you wanted これ.

は vs が — the first pass

Both can mark what looks like a subject, and the difference takes the whole book to fully own — but the day-one version is genuinely simple:

は — old information

"As for X (which we both already have in mind), here's the news about it." The news is what comes after は.

私わたしは・エンジニアです。
As for me → the news: engineer.

が — new information

"X (and specifically X, this is the news itself) did/is Y." The news is the word before が. Question words are always new info, so they always take が.

誰だれが来きますか。— 田中たなかさんが来ます。

Test yourself with the spotlight trick: が puts the spotlight on what's before it; は puts the spotlight on what's after it. "WHO came?" → spotlight on who → が. "What do you do?" → spotlight on the answer part → は. We'll return to this in JP-08 and JP-15 as sentences get richer.

The politeness dial exists — set it to です・ます for now

Everything in Parts I–II is in the polite です・ます style: correct with strangers, colleagues, shopkeepers, everyone. The casual "plain form" arrives in JP-09 — where you'll discover it's not just casual speech, it's the gear that drives all complex grammar. One dial, learn the polite setting first.

Genki's greeting notes — the four things learners actually mix up

おはよう vs おはようございます: Genki's rule of thumb — if you're on a first-name basis, the short form is fine; if you'd address them as Mr./Ms., use the full ございます version. Same split for ありがとう/ありがとうございます. Students use the long forms with teachers, always.

すみません does three jobs: "excuse me" (getting attention), "I'm sorry" (apologizing), and "thank you" (for trouble taken on your behalf). The third use confuses everyone — a Japanese person thanking you with an apology is completely normal.

さようなら is heavier than "goodbye". It implies real separation — not seeing someone until a new chapter. For everyday partings use じゃあ、また (see you) with friends, or しつれいします when leaving a teacher's or boss's room.

The home ritual pairs: いってきます ↔ いってらっしゃい (leaving home), ただいま ↔ おかえりなさい (returning), いただきます / ごちそうさまでした (bracketing every meal). These are exchanges, not words — learn each pair as one unit.

Vocabulary — people & introductions

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — build the sentences

Assemble each sentence from its scrambled pieces. Say it aloud before you click.

JP-03

Particles I — を・に・で・へ・と・も・の

Seven one-syllable words that do the work English does with word order and prepositions. Learn each particle's core image, and every sentence in the book becomes parseable.

The case particles, by core image

ParticleCore imageExample
をArrow into the verb — the thing acted onパンを食たべます (eat bread)
にPin on the map — destination, target, point in time, location of existence東京とうきょうに行いきます (go to Tokyo)・七時しちじに (at 7:00)
でStage & tool — where an action happens, or what it's done withカフェで働はたらきます (work at a café)・ペンで書かきます (write with a pen)
へDirection of travel (pronounced e) — softer, "toward"駅えきへ行いきます (head toward the station)
とTogether-with; also "and" for complete lists友達ともだちと映画えいがを見みます (watch a movie with a friend)
も"Also/too" — replaces は・が・を私わたしも行いきます (I'll go too)
のPossession/attachment — A の B = "B of A"私わたしの本ほん (my book)・日本にほんの車くるま (Japanese cars)

に vs で — the pin and the stage

Both translate as "in/at", which is exactly why they're the first real particle decision you'll sweat. The distinction is about the verb, not the place:

に — where something IS or ARRIVES

Existence and destination verbs: いる・ある・住すむ・行いく・来くる・着つく・入はいる.

大阪おおさかに住すんでいます。
I live in Osaka. (pinned there)

で — where something HAPPENS

Action verbs: 食たべる・買かう・働はたらく・会あう・勉強べんきょうする.

レストランで食たべます。
I eat at a restaurant. (stage for the action)

Mental model

Ask "is the place a pin (endpoint, existence) or a stage (venue for activity)?" 東京に行く — Tokyo is the pin the arrow lands on. 東京で買う — Tokyo is the stage where buying happens. Verbs of being and arriving take に; verbs of doing take で.

Time expressions: when に, when nothing

Clock/calendar times take に: 三時さんじに (at 3:00), 月曜日げつようびに (on Monday), 二月にがつに (in February). Relative times take nothing: 今日きょう・明日あした・毎日まいにち・今いま stand bare. Rule of thumb: if you could point to it on a clock or calendar, pin it with に.

毎日まいにち七時しちじに起おきます。Every day I wake at 7:00. (毎日 bare, 七時 pinned)

の — the possessive chain

AのB reads right-to-left as "B of A", and chains freely:

私わたしの会社かいしゃの人ひとa person from my company (person ← of company ← of me)

の also covers material and category: 日本語にほんごの本ほん "a Japanese-language book". When you meet a long のののchain in the wild, parse from the last noun backward — the final noun is always the actual thing.

も — the substitution rule

も means "also", and it replaces は, が, or を (never stacks on them): 私も行きます, not ×私はも. With other particles it stacks after: 東京とうきょうにも行いきます "I'll go to Tokyo too."

Vocabulary — places & movement

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — choose the particle

The instrument below gives you a sentence with a hole in it. Pick the particle, get told exactly why you're right or wrong. Aim for a clean 10.

JP-04

Verbs I — three groups, the ます line

Every Japanese verb belongs to one of three groups, and once you can name the group, you can conjugate anything the language will ever throw at you. This is the single highest-leverage skill in the book.

The three groups

Textbooks call them Group 1/2/3, う-verbs and る-verbs, or godan/ichidan. Same thing:

Group 1 — 五段 (u-verbs)

The big group. Dictionary form ends in an -u sound: 書かく・飲のむ・買かう・話はなす・待まつ. Conjugates by sliding the last kana along its consonant row: く→き→か→こ→け.

Group 2 — 一段 (ru-verbs)

Ends in -iru or -eru: 食たべる・見みる・起おきる・寝ねる. Conjugates by dropping る and attaching endings to the naked stem. The easy group.

Group 3 — irregular

Exactly two: する (do) and 来くる (come). Memorize their forms as vocabulary. する matters enormously because noun + する creates thousands of verbs: 勉強べんきょうする (study), 電話でんわする (phone).

The trap: fake る-verbs

Ending in -eru/-iru makes a verb look like Group 2, but a stubborn minority are Group 1: 帰かえる (return), 入はいる (enter), 走はしる (run), 知しる (know), 切きる (cut). Test: put it in ます-form. 帰る→帰ります (slides → Group 1); 食べる→食べます (drops る → Group 2). Every vocab table in this book marks the group so you never have to guess.

The ます line — polite non-past and friends

Take the stem (Group 1: slide final kana to its い-row sound; Group 2: drop る) and attach the polite endings. Like です, it's a 2×2 grid:

VerbGroupNon-pastNegativePastPast negative
書かく write1書きます書きません書きました書きませんでした
飲のむ drink1飲みます飲みません飲みました飲みませんでした
食たべる eat2食べます食べません食べました食べませんでした
する do3しますしませんしましたしませんでした
来くる come3来ます（きます）来ません来ました来ませんでした

Remember: non-past covers present habit and future. 明日あした、行いきます = "I'll go tomorrow." No separate future tense exists, ever. One less thing.

Full sentences now

私わたしは毎朝まいあさコーヒーを飲のみます。I drink coffee every morning.
昨日きのう、映画えいがを見みました。Yesterday I watched a movie.
肉にくを食たべません。I don't eat meat.

Note the standard rhythm: topic は → time → place → object を → verb. Not a law (particles carry the roles), but it's the neutral order your ear should expect.

Invitations: ませんか and ましょう

一緒いっしょに昼ひるごはんを食たべませんか。Won't you have lunch with me? (invitation — negative question softens it)
行いきましょう。Let's go.

〜ませんか invites ("won't you…?"), 〜ましょう proposes ("let's…"). Both are just the ます line with a different tail — a pattern to get used to: Japanese grammar is overwhelmingly tails on stems.

Conjugate for yourself

The stepper below shows the derivation, kana by kana, for any verb in this book's core list. Watch Group 1 slide and Group 2 snap. Ten minutes here saves ten weeks later.

Vocabulary — 45 core verbs

These verbs cover the bulk of daily life and recur in every later chapter. Group is marked; drill until group-recognition is instant.

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

JP-05

Adjectives — い and な, and their conjugations

Japanese adjectives conjugate like verbs — they carry their own tense and negation. There are two clans, and everything depends on telling them apart.

Two clans

い-adjectives

End in い and conjugate by swapping the い: 高たかい・安やすい・面白おもしろい・寒さむい. True Japanese natives — they need no です to be a sentence (です is added only for politeness).

な-adjectives

Behave like nouns; they take な before a noun and conjugate with です/じゃない: 静しずか・有名ゆうめい・便利べんり・元気げんき. Mostly Chinese-derived. Grammatically, they're nouns wearing an adjective costume.

Modify a nounNon-pastNegativePastPast negative
高たかい高い店みせ高いです高くないです高かったです高くなかったです
静しずか静かな町まち静かです静かじゃないです静かでした静かじゃなかったです
Two famous landmines

1) いい (good) conjugates from its older form よい: 良くない・良かった — never ×いくない・×いかった.

2) Past tense lives inside the adjective, never in です: 高かったです, not ×高いでした. The adjective conjugates; です just sits there being polite.

The impostors

きれい (pretty/clean) and 有名ゆうめい (famous) end in い but are な-adjectives: きれいな花はな, きれいじゃない. The い is part of the kanji reading (綺麗・有名), not a conjugating tail. 嫌きらい (disliked) is the third impostor.

Adverbs: く and に

い-adjectives become adverbs with く, な-adjectives with に: 早はやく起おきます (wake early), 静しずかに話はなします (speak quietly). Degree words stack in front: とても (very), ちょっと (a bit), あまり〜ない (not very — needs a negative), 全然ぜんぜん〜ない (not at all — needs a negative).

この店みせはあまり高たかくないです。This shop isn't very expensive.

Liking and wanting are adjectives

Japanese expresses "like", "dislike", "good at", "bad at" with な-adjectives, and the liked thing takes が:

私わたしは猫ねこが好すきです。I like cats. (lit. "as for me, cats are liked")
兄あには料理りょうりが上手じょうずです。My brother is good at cooking.

This は…が… "double subject" shape (topic + spotlight) is everywhere in Japanese — file it now, we'll lean on it hard in JP-08 and JP-15.

Vocabulary — 45 core adjectives

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — conjugate & choose

JP-06

Numbers, time & counters

Japanese numbers are beautifully regular — until you attach them to things, at which point sound changes and counter words turn arithmetic into phonology. Drill it now; it never gets drilled by accident.

The number system: build from 14 pieces

Learn 1–10, then 百・千・万, and you can say any number to 99,999,999. It's purely positional: 342 = 三百四十二 "three-hundred four-ten two".

1–10一 いち・二 に・三 さん・四 よん/し・五 ご・六 ろく・七 なな/しち・八 はち・九 きゅう/く・十 じゅう
Powers百 ひゃく (100)・千 せん (1,000)・万 まん (10,000)
Sound changes300 さんびゃく・600 ろっぴゃく・800 はっぴゃく・3000 さんぜん・8000 はっせん・いっぷん/さんぷん…
The 万 wall

Japanese groups large numbers by 10,000 (万), not 1,000. 100,000 is 十万 "ten ten-thousands"; 1,000,000 is 百万 "hundred ten-thousands". When you hear a big number, mentally chunk it in fours from the right, not threes. This one habit is the difference between following prices in Japan and nodding blankly.

Time

Hours〜時（じ）: 一時・二時…　watch: 四時 よじ・七時 しちじ・九時 くじ
Minutes〜分（ふん/ぷん）: 一分 いっぷん・三分 さんぷん・十分 じゅっぷん
Days of week月・火・水・木・金・土・日 ＋ 曜日（ようび）
Half past〜時半（はん）: 七時半 = 7:30
会議かいぎは月曜日げつようびの十時じゅうじからです。The meeting is from 10:00 on Monday. (から = from, まで = until)

Counters: numbers must wear a uniform

You can't count bare in Japanese — the number takes a suffix that classifies the shape/type of the thing. The essential seven:

CounterCountsWatch out for
〜つgeneric things (1–10, native numbers)ひとつ・ふたつ・みっつ・よっつ・いつつ・むっつ・ななつ・やっつ・ここのつ・とお
〜人（にん）people一人 ひとり・二人 ふたり・三人 さんにん…
〜本（ほん）long thin things (bottles, pens, trains, calls)一本 いっぽん・三本 さんぼん・六本 ろっぽん
〜枚（まい）flat things (paper, shirts, tickets)regular, blessedly
〜匹（ひき）small animals一匹 いっぴき・三匹 さんびき・六匹 ろっぴき
〜個（こ）small compact objects一個 いっこ・六個 ろっこ
〜回（かい）occurrences, times一回 いっかい・六回 ろっかい

Word order: ビールを二本にほん買かいました — the counter floats after the particle, before the verb. When you don't know the right counter, 〜つ (or 〜個) is the socially acceptable fallback for objects.

Drill the machine

The trainer below throws random numbers, prices, times and counter questions at you, and shows the reading with all sound changes worked out. This is pure reps territory — five minutes a day for two weeks and it's yours for life.

Vocabulary — time words

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

JP-07

て-form — the hinge of Japanese

One conjugation unlocks requests, permission, prohibition, ongoing actions, and chaining sentences. If you drill exactly one thing from Part II, drill this.

Formation: the て song

Group 2 is trivial: drop る, add て (食べる→食べて). Group 3: する→して, 来る→来きて. Group 1 splits by the final kana — this is the table generations of students have chanted:

Dictionary ends inて-formExample
う・つ・る→ って買かう→買って・待まつ→待って・帰かえる→帰って
む・ぶ・ぬ→ んで飲のむ→飲んで・遊あそぶ→遊んで・死しぬ→死んで
く→ いて書かく→書いて　(exception: 行いく→行って)
ぐ→ いで泳およぐ→泳いで
す→ して話はなす→話して

Drill it until it's reflex — the stepper shows every derivation:

What て buys you

1. Requests — てください

ちょっと待まってください。Please wait a moment.

2. Ongoing action & ongoing states — ている

今いま、昼ひるごはんを食たべています。I'm eating lunch right now. (action in progress)
結婚けっこんしています。I am married. (resulting state — not "am marrying"!)

ている covers both "-ing" and "state resulting from a change". Verbs of instantaneous change (結婚する・住すむ・知しる・持もつ) use ている for the state: 知っています = "I know", 東京に住んでいます = "I live in Tokyo". Which flavor applies is a property of the verb — your vocab tables note the state-verbs.

3. Permission and prohibition

写真しゃしんを撮とってもいいですか。May I take a photo? (てもいい = it's fine even if you…)
ここでタバコを吸すってはいけません。You must not smoke here. (てはいけない = prohibition)

4. Chaining actions — the "and then" glue

朝あさ起おきて、シャワーを浴あびて、会社かいしゃに行いきます。I get up, shower, and go to work. (only the final verb carries tense/politeness)
Mental model

て is a hinge, not a meaning. It leaves the verb "open", waiting for the next thing — a request (ください), a state (いる), a judgment (もいい／はいけない), or simply the next verb. When you meet a new 〜て◯◯ pattern later (and you will, constantly), read it as "verb, hinged into ◯◯".

Vocabulary — the daily routine

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — て-form under fire

JP-08

Existence & location — いる・ある

"There is" splits in two based on whether the thing breathes. Add position words and から/まで, and you can describe where anything is — the second most useful skill for surviving Japan.

ある for things, いる for the living

ある — inanimate existence

机つくえの上うえに本ほんがあります。
There's a book on the desk.

Negative: ありません. Plain form: ある／ない (yes, the negative is just ない).

いる — animate existence

公園こうえんに子供こどもがいます。
There are children in the park.

People and animals. Negative: いません. Plain: いる／いない.

The pattern is PLACE に THING が あります/います. Flip it to say where something is: トイレは二階にかいにあります "The toilet is on the second floor" — known thing becomes は-topic, place keeps its に.

ある also means "to have"

時間じかんがありません "I have no time." 兄弟きょうだいが二人ふたりいます "I have two siblings." Japanese possession is just existence in your sphere — no "have" verb needed.

The position words

Position words are nouns, connected with の: AのB に = "at the B of A".

上うえ above/on下した below/under中なか inside外そと outside
前まえ in front後うしろ behind隣となり next to近ちかく near
右みぎ right左ひだり left間あいだ between向むかい across from
銀行ぎんこうは駅えきの前まえにあります。The bank is in front of the station.
猫ねこは箱はこの中なかにいます。The cat is in the box. (of course it is)

から・まで — from and until

会社かいしゃは九時くじから五時ごじまでです。Work is from 9 to 5.
家いえから駅えきまで十分じゅっぷんかかります。It takes 10 minutes from home to the station. (かかる = to take time/cost)

から/まで work for both space and time, and attach directly to the noun. You now have the full toolkit for directions, schedules, and describing any scene.

Vocabulary — home & everyday objects

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — describe the scene

JP-09

Plain form — the real engine of the language

You learned です・ます first for politeness. Now meet the forms underneath — not "casual slang" but the gears every complex sentence is built from.

The four plain forms

VerbNon-pastNegativePastPast negative
書かく (G1)書く書かない書いた書かなかった
飲のむ (G1)飲む飲まない飲んだ飲まなかった
食たべる (G2)食べる食べない食べた食べなかった
する (G3)するしないしたしなかった
来くる (G3)来る来こない来きた来なかった

Three mechanical facts: the た-form is the て-form with た (書いて→書いた, 飲んで→飲んだ — the same sound-change table, for free). The negative ない attaches to the あ-row for Group 1 (書く→書か+ない), with one wrinkle: う-ending verbs go to わ (買う→買わない). And ない itself conjugates like an い-adjective (なかった), which is why past-negative looks the way it does.

Why plain form matters more than politeness

Plain form is what goes inside larger structures. Politeness lives only at the very end of a sentence; everything embedded within is plain. Three patterns you can use immediately:

と思おもいます — I think that…

明日あしたは雨あめが降ふると思います。I think it will rain tomorrow. (降る = plain, politeness carried by 思います)

と言いいました — said that…

田中さんは来こないと言いました。Tanaka said he's not coming.

〜たことがあります — have you ever…

日本にほんに行いったことがありますか。Have you ever been to Japan? (lit. "does the experience of having-gone exist?")
Mental model

Think of です・ます as an outer coat the sentence puts on at the door. Inside the house — inside quotes, inside "I think", inside clauses that modify nouns (JP-15), inside conditionals — everyone walks around in plain form. From this chapter on, every vocab drill shows verbs in dictionary form, because that's their true name.

Casual speech, briefly

Between friends, the plain form IS the sentence: 食べる？ "eating?" — 食べた "ate." Questions drop か and just rise in pitch; だ replaces です (but is usually dropped by women and often by everyone: これ、おいしいね). Sentence-final ね seeks agreement ("right?"), よ asserts new info ("I'm telling you"). Don't practice casual on strangers yet — but recognize it, because all native media runs on it.

Vocabulary — food & drink

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — plain-form gymnastics

JP-10

Joining & reasoning — から・ので・けど・そして

Single sentences become paragraphs. Cause, contrast, and sequence — plus んです, the "explanation mode" that makes your Japanese suddenly sound human.

Because: から and ので

時間じかんがないから、タクシーで行いきます。There's no time, so I'll go by taxi.
雨あめなので、家いえにいます。Since it's raining, I'll stay home.

Both mean "because", and the reason comes first (Japanese states cause before effect — reverse of the English default). Nuance: から is direct, subjective; ので is softer, more objective — preferred for excuses and polite requests. Grammar detail: ので needs な after nouns and な-adjectives (雨なので・便利なので); から takes だ (雨だから).

But: が and けど

すみませんが、駅えきはどこですか。Excuse me, but where is the station? (softening が)
高たかいけど、買かいます。It's expensive, but I'll buy it.

Sentence-joining が (formal) and けど (everyday) both mean "but" — and both also serve as a soft connector that isn't adversative at all, just clearing the throat before the main point. Half of real conversations start with 〜ですけど…

Sentence starters

そしてand then, and alsoでもbut (starts a sentence)
それからafter thatだからtherefore
たとえばfor exampleところでby the way

Incomplete lists: や and とか

と lists exhaustively ("A and B, that's all"); や lists by example ("A and B, among others"): 寿司すしやラーメンを食たべました "I ate sushi, ramen, and such." とか is its casual cousin.

んです — explanation mode

Add んです (plain form + んです; nouns/な-adj insert な) and a bare statement becomes an explanation — offering or seeking background:

どうして遅おくれたんですか。Why were you late? (probing for the story)
電車でんしゃが止とまったんです。(It's that) the train stopped. (giving the story)
Why んです matters

Without it, answers sound like database rows. 頭が痛いです states a fact; 頭が痛いんです says "here's the situation (so please understand/act accordingly)". Questions with んですか show interest; answers with んです give context. It is the most common piece of grammar that textbooks under-teach and natives use in every third sentence.

Vocabulary — feelings & character

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — connect the clauses

LN-01

Listening I — numbers, times, prices by ear

A third of the JLPT is listening, and the single most common N5 listening task is catching a number, a time, or a price at native speed. This gym generates infinite practice.

How to train

The instrument speaks; you pick what you heard. No text appears until you answer — that's the point. Numbers are where every learner's ear lags their eye, because the sound changes (さんびゃく、ろっぴゃく、いっぷん) that you learned in JP-06 arrive fused at full speed. Fifteen minutes here, three days in a row, and the fog lifts noticeably.

This gym needs the Japanese voice

The listening instruments use your device's Japanese text-to-speech. If the Sound Check in JP-00 isn't green yet, fix that first (or open this file in Edge). Everything else in the book works without audio — the listening gyms are the one part that genuinely can't.

Ear-training rules

  • No replays on the first guess. The exam plays everything once. Train the way you'll fight.
  • Say what you heard aloud before answering. Shadowing the audio doubles the encoding and builds your own pronunciation for free.
  • Wrong twice on the same pattern? Go re-run the number trainer in JP-06 with your eyes open, then come back.
LN-02

Listening II — mini-dialogues

Two lines of conversation, one question — the exact shape of the JLPT's 課題理解 listening tasks, built from grammar you already own.

The N5 listening formula

Almost every N5 dialogue question follows one script: a situation is named, two people exchange 2–4 short lines, and the question asks what will the person do / where / when / which one. The traps are always the same three: a corrected statement (「じゃあ、三時？」「いいえ、四時です」— the answer is the correction), a declined offer, or two similar numbers. Listen for the second mention of anything — corrections come second.

After each dialogue

Replay it (allowed here, not on question one) and read along with the revealed transcript, aloud. This turns every quiz item into shadowing material. Ten dialogues shadowed daily is a genuine listening course in miniature.

LN-03

Listening III — phrases you'll hear all day

Station announcements, classroom instructions, shop keigo — the ambient Japanese of daily life, drilled by ear so it stops being noise.

Why phrase-listening is its own skill

These phrases arrive as single fused sounds, not word-by-word: いらっしゃいませ, もういちどおねがいします, でんしゃがまいります. You don't parse them — you recognize them whole, like song hooks. The JLPT tests a set called 即時応答 ("instant response"): you hear a phrase and pick the natural reply. The drill below mixes both: what does it mean, and what do you say back.

Free immersion, starting tonight

Your listening gym has a bigger sibling: any anime/drama you already like, Japanese audio, one scene rewatched three times. First pass: just listen. Second: Japanese subtitles. Third: shadow one line you liked. Fifteen minutes, most days. This plus the gyms is a complete N5 listening prep.

RD-01

Reading I — signs, notes, notices

N5 reading starts with 100-character texts: a store's hours, a note on a door, a friend's message. Short, real, and exactly what these five texts train.

The N5 reading method

Read the text once without stopping, even where you're lost. Then answer. Then hover the furigana and re-read. The exam gives you ~1 minute per short text; training your tolerance for partial understanding IS the skill. Romaji is on for these if you need it — but try each text kanji-first; every kanji here is from the KJ chapters.

テキスト 1 — 店の看板

スーパーまるいち
あさ九時くじから夜よる八時はちじまで
水曜日すいようびは休やすみですMaruichi Supermarket — 9 a.m. to 8 p.m. Closed Wednesdays.

テキスト 2 — ドアのメモ

田中たなかさんへ
三時さんじに来きました。いませんでしたから、また明日あした来きます。電話でんわをください。
山田To Tanaka-san — I came at 3:00. You weren't here, so I'll come again tomorrow. Please call me. — Yamada

テキスト 3 — 駅のお知らせ

南口みなみぐちは今いま使つかえません。東口ひがしぐちを使つかってください。The south exit cannot be used right now. Please use the east exit.

テキスト 4 — 友達からのメッセージ

あしたのパーティー、六時ろくじからです。飲のみ物ものを買かって来きてください。食たべ物ものはありますから、買かわなくてもいいです。Tomorrow's party starts at 6:00. Please buy and bring drinks. There's food, so you don't need to buy any.

テキスト 5 — 教室で

テストは金曜日きんようびです。本ほんを見みてはいけません。えんぴつで書かいてください。The test is Friday. You may not look at your books. Please write in pencil.

Comprehension check

RD-02

Reading II — short passages

The 4–6 sentence passage: a self-introduction, someone's daily routine, a postcard. The N5 exam's medium-length texts, at exam difficulty.

読み物 1 — 自己紹介

はじめまして。リサです。アメリカから来きました。今いま、東京とうきょうの大学だいがくで日本語にほんごを勉強べんきょうしています。毎朝まいあさ七時しちじに起おきて、電車でんしゃで学校がっこうに行いきます。日本にほんの食たべ物ものが大好だいすきです。でも、なっとうはちょっと……。Nice to meet you, I'm Lisa. I came from America. I'm studying Japanese at a university in Tokyo now. Every morning I get up at 7:00 and go to school by train. I love Japanese food. But nattō is… well…

読み物 2 — 山田さんの一日

山田やまださんは銀行ぎんこうで働はたらいています。月曜日げつようびから金曜日きんようびまで、九時くじから五時ごじまで仕事しごとです。昼ひるごはんはいつも会社かいしゃの近ちかくの店みせで食たべます。週末しゅうまつは山やまに行いったり、本ほんを読よんだりします。Yamada-san works at a bank. Monday to Friday, work is 9:00 to 5:00. He always eats lunch at a shop near the office. On weekends he does things like go to the mountains and read books.

読み物 3 — はがき

おげんきですか。私わたしは今いま京都きょうとにいます。きのうはお寺てらを見みました。とてもきれいでした。京都きょうとの食たべ物ものはおいしいですが、少すこし高たかいです。明日あした、東京とうきょうに帰かえります。おみやげを買かいました。楽たのしみにしていてください。How are you? I'm in Kyoto now. Yesterday I saw temples — they were beautiful. Kyoto's food is delicious but a little expensive. Tomorrow I return to Tokyo. I bought souvenirs — look forward to them!

Comprehension check

RD-03

Reading III — the N5→N4 bridge

Two texts slightly above exam level. If these feel merely "hard" rather than impossible, you are ready for the real thing — examiners under-shoot what you trained on.

読み物 1 — メール

けんたさん
こんにちは。来週らいしゅうの土曜日どようび、友達ともだちと海うみに行いきます。けんたさんも一緒いっしょに行いきませんか。朝あさ八時はちじに駅えきの北口きたぐちで会あいましょう。電車でんしゃで一時間いちじかんぐらいかかります。お弁当べんとうを持もって来きてください。飲のみ物ものは私わたしが買かいます。天気てんきが悪わるかったら、映画えいがを見みに行いきましょう。
まりKenta — hi! Next Saturday I'm going to the beach with friends. Won't you come too? Let's meet at 8 a.m. at the station's north exit. It takes about an hour by train. Please bring a boxed lunch — I'll buy the drinks. If the weather is bad, let's go see a movie instead. — Mari

読み物 2 — 小さな話

私わたしの祖母そぼは七十八歳ななじゅうはっさいです。毎朝まいあさ五時ごじに起おきて、犬いぬと散歩さんぽします。雨あめの日ひも行いきます。「犬いぬが待まっていますから」と言いいます。祖母そぼはスマホを使つかうのが上手じょうずで、毎日まいにち写真しゃしんを送おくってくれます。今朝けさの写真しゃしんは川かわの近ちかくの花はなでした。私わたしも早はやく起おきて、祖母そぼと歩あるきたいと思おもいます。でも五時ごじは早はやすぎます。My grandmother is 78. Every morning she gets up at five and walks the dog — rainy days too. "The dog is waiting," she says. She's good with her smartphone and sends me a photo every day; this morning's was of flowers near the river. I think I'd like to get up early and walk with her too. But five o'clock is too early.

Comprehension check

VB-01

Conjugation bootcamp

A drill-only chapter. No new grammar — just the four verb forms N5 tests, cycled until your mouth answers before your brain does.

The circuit

Run each station below to 8/10. The quiz button inside the stepper generates fresh questions every run, so this chapter never wears out. Order matters — it's easiest to hardest:

  1. ます-form (polite line) — the form every N5 listening track speaks in.
  2. て-form — requests, permissions, and the glue of every compound tense.
  3. Plain past た — free if your て-form is solid (same sound rules).
  4. Plain negative ない — watch the う→わ verbs (買う→買わない).

Station 1 & 2 — ます and て

Station 3 & 4 — た and ない

The 30-second daily ritual

After bootcamp is "done", keep it alive with one habit: every day when the Dojo shows you a verb, silently run its four forms before answering. 食べる → 食べます・食べて・食べた・食べない. Five seconds per verb, forever. This is what "knowing conjugation" actually feels like from the inside.

N5-01

The Road to N5 — a 70-day plan

Everything in this book, sequenced into ten weeks. Follow it honestly at 60–90 minutes a day and the exam becomes a formality.

The plan

DaysNew materialDaily maintenance
1–7KA-01 → KA-05: both kana sets + typing. One chapter every day or two; re-run yesterday's quiz before today's.Kana quizzes to 12/15
8–21KJ-01 → KJ-05 (one set per ~3 days) interleaved with JP-01 → JP-03. Kanji in the morning, grammar in the evening beats both at once.Dojo + one kanji drill
22–35JP-04 → JP-08: verbs, adjectives, numbers, て-form, existence. This is the heart of N5 grammar — go slower here if you go slower anywhere.Dojo + VB-01 circuit
36–45JP-09 → JP-10 + LN-01/LN-02 daily. Plain form and reasons finish the N5 grammar list.Dojo + one listening set
46–55RD-01 → RD-03 + LN-03. Reading volume phase — re-read old passages for speed, not just new ones for coverage.Dojo + shadowing
56–63Review sweep: re-run every chapter drill that's below 8/10. The Dojo's misses tell you exactly where.Full-book Dojo (JP-30)
64–70N5-02 mock exam every second day. Between mocks: fix what each mock exposes. Nothing new after day 63.Light Dojo + sleep
The honest arithmetic — pick your track

Zero-to-N5 is commonly 250–350 hours without a kanji head start. Two honest tracks: 70 days needs ~60–90 min/day (~80–100 h — tight but real); 30 min/day lands N5 in about 5 months (~75 h — the floor, with little slack). Either works — the order never changes, only the calendar. Two cautions at the low-time end: listening is the first skill to starve (protect the LN gyms and real-media time), and zero days break streaks faster than slow days. The Dojo's spaced repetition is built to make short daily sessions count.

Coverage map — is every pattern covered?

Beyond this file

Three additions make the plan stronger, all free: Tofugu's roadmap for method sanity-checks; official JLPT sample questions (jlpt.jp) in week 9; and any show you love with Japanese audio, fifteen minutes daily from week 4. If you have Genki I, the chips on every chapter tell you which lesson to read alongside.

N5-02

Mock exam — walk through the first gate

Thirty questions sampled from everything you've studied, scored by section like the real exam. Pass bar: 60%. The real N5 pass bar is lower — you're training with a heavier sword.

Exam-day mechanics (read once)

  • The real N5 is 90 minutes, three timed sections (official structure, jlpt.jp): Language Knowledge — Vocabulary 20 min; Language Knowledge — Grammar & Reading 40 min; Listening 30 min. Scored 0–180; pass is ~80/180 and you must clear each section\u2019s minimum — one zeroed section fails you regardless of total.
  • Never leave a blank. No penalty for guessing. Eliminate one option, guess from three.
  • Reading section: read the question first, then hunt the text for its answer. N5 texts are short enough to scan.
  • Listening: the answer is usually the second version of any repeated detail (corrections!). Mark and move on — the next track waits for no one.

The mock

After each mock

Your section scores are the diagnosis: vocab low → Dojo twice daily for a week; grammar low → re-run the quiz of the specific chapters you missed (the mock names them); reading low → re-read RD passages for speed; listening low → LN-01 daily. Then mock again in 48 hours. Two consecutive passes above 70% and you should register for the real thing — momentum is a resource, spend it. 頑張がんばって！

JP-11

Wanting & intending — たい・ほしい・つもり

Desire, plans, and "let's" — the grammar of having a will of your own, plus the strange rule about other people's feelings.

たい — wanting to do

Verb stem (the ます-stem) + たい, and the result conjugates as an い-adjective:

日本にほんに行いきたいです。I want to go to Japan.
今日きょうは何なにもしたくないです。Today I don't want to do anything. (たい→たくない, adjective rules)

The object may take を or が: 水が飲みたい is slightly more idiomatic than 水を飲みたい. For things rather than actions, use ほしい (also an い-adjective): 新あたらしいパソコンがほしいです "I want a new computer."

Other people's insides are off-limits

たい and ほしい state your own inner state. For third parties, Japanese grammatically refuses direct access to minds: use 〜たがっている／ほしがっている ("shows signs of wanting") or hedge with 〜と言っていました. ×妹はケーキを食べたいです → ○妹はケーキを食べたがっています. This "evidentiality" runs through the whole language — you'll meet it again with そう/らしい in JP-18.

つもり — plans, and the volitional — "let's/shall I"

来年らいねん、日本にほんで働はたらくつもりです。I plan to work in Japan next year. (plain form + つもり)

The plain volitional form ("let's…" to yourself or peers) is: Group 1 → お-row + う (行く→行こう・飲む→飲もう); Group 2 → よう (食べよう); する→しよう, 来る→来こよう. Add と思っています and you get the everyday way to state intentions:

今年ことしこそN3を受うけようと思っています。This year for sure, I'm thinking of taking the N3.

たり〜たりする — doing things like…

週末しゅうまつは映画えいがを見みたり、本ほんを読よんだりします。On weekends I do things like watch movies and read. (た-form + り, close with する)

Like や for nouns, たり lists actions non-exhaustively. Common error: forgetting the final する, which carries the tense.

Vocabulary — shopping & money

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習

JP-12

Can, must, may — potential & obligation

Ability, necessity, and permission — the modal triangle that turns you from a describer of events into a negotiator of them.

The potential form — "can"

Group 1: slide to え-row + る (書く→書ける・飲む→飲める). Group 2: られる (食べられる — colloquially 食べれる, the famous "ら抜き"). する→できる, 来る→来られる. The result is a Group 2 verb, and the object usually switches を→が:

漢字かんじが読よめます。I can read kanji. (you, soon, honestly)
納豆なっとうは食たべられません。I can't eat nattō.

Alternative: dictionary form + ことができる (泳ぐことができます) — stiffer, common in writing.

Must: the なければ family

"Must" in Japanese is literally "if you don't do it, it won't do" — negative stem + a conditional + a bad-outcome word. The full form and its street versions:

FormRegisterExample
〜なければなりませんformal行かなければなりません
〜なければいけない／〜なきゃspoken行かなきゃ (gotta go)
〜ないといけないspoken, very commonもう帰かえらないといけない

"Don't have to" is 〜なくてもいいです ("it's fine even not doing"): 明日あしたは来こなくてもいいですよ "You don't need to come tomorrow." Compare the permission/prohibition pair from JP-07 — the whole modal system is just て/なくて + もいい/はいけない combinations.

Too much, easy, hard — stem suffixes

〜すぎるtoo much食たべすぎました (ate too much)・高たかすぎる (too expensive)
〜やすいeasy toこの本ほんは読よみやすい (easy to read)
〜にくいhard toこの薬くすりは飲のみにくい (hard to swallow)

All three attach to the ます-stem and conjugate as their ending suggests (すぎる as a Group 2 verb, やすい/にくい as い-adjectives). Stems are sockets; suffixes are plugs.

Vocabulary — work & study

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習

JP-13

Giving & receiving — あげる・くれる・もらう

Three verbs for "give/receive" that encode whose side you're on. English doesn't have this axis at all — which is exactly why it needs deliberate drilling.

The triangle

あげる

Giving outward — I (or my side) give to others; others give to others.

私わたしは友達ともだちに本ほんをあげました。
I gave my friend a book.

くれる

Giving inward — someone gives to me/my side. The giver takes が/は, and the fact that it's くれる (not あげる) already tells you the receiver is "us".

田中たなかさんが切符きっぷをくれました。
Tanaka gave me a ticket.

もらう

Receiving — subject is the receiver; the source takes に (or から).

友達ともだちに切符きっぷをもらいました。
I received a ticket from a friend.

Mental model: the camera

Japanese narration has a camera fixed at "my side" (うち). あげる films a gift moving away from the camera, くれる films one moving toward it, もらう films the catch. Choosing the verb IS choosing the camera angle — that's why 「田中さんが私にあげた」 sounds broken: it films an incoming gift with the outgoing camera.

The favor versions: 〜てあげる・〜てくれる・〜てもらう

Hinge any verb with て into the triangle and actions become favors — with the same directionality:

荷物にもつを持もってあげました。I carried the bags (for someone, as a favor).
先生せんせいが漢字かんじを教おしえてくれました。The teacher (kindly) taught me kanji.
友達ともだちに手伝てつだってもらいました。I had a friend help me / a friend helped me out.

てくれる is how gratitude enters ordinary sentences — dropping it makes you sound oddly entitled ("the teacher taught me" as a cold fact). Politeness upgrades: あげる→さしあげる, くれる→くださる, もらう→いただく (bank these; they return in the keigo chapter).

Family words: two sets

Humble set for your own family when talking to others (父ちち・母はは・兄あに・姉あね); honorific set for other people's (お父とうさん・お母かあさん・お兄にいさん・お姉ねえさん). Same うち/そと camera as the giving verbs — your side gets humble words, their side gets raised words. (Within the family, everyone uses the honorific set as address: kids call their own mom お母さん.)

Vocabulary — family & people

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — pick the right verb

JP-14

Comparing & becoming — より・ほう・なる

Japanese has no "-er" or "-est". Comparison is done with word order and two nouns — ほう (side) and いちばん (number one) — plus なる, the verb of change that English keeps splitting into a dozen phrases.

A より B — comparing two

新幹線しんかんせんはバスより速はやいです。The Shinkansen is faster than the bus. (より = "than"; adjective stays plain)
コーヒーよりお茶ちゃのほうが好すきです。I like tea more than coffee. (のほうが marks the winner explicitly)

Asking which of two: コーヒーとお茶と、どちらが好きですか — answer with 〜のほうが. Note there's no change to the adjective itself; "faster" is just 速い with a より somewhere in the sentence.

いちばん — superlatives

日本にほんで一番いちばん高たかい山やまは富士山ふじさんです。The highest mountain in Japan is Mt. Fuji. (group + で + 一番 + adjective)

Question form: スポーツの中なかで何なにが一番好きですか "Among sports, which do you like best?" — the category takes (の中)で.

なる — the verb of becoming

English says get cold, turn red, go bad, become a doctor, grow stronger. Japanese says なる for all of it, with the adverb forms from JP-05:

AttachPatternExample
い-adjective〜く なる寒さむくなりました (it got cold)
な-adjective〜に なる日本語にほんごが上手じょうずになりました (got good at Japanese)
noun〜に なるエンジニアになりました (became an engineer)
なる thinking

Japanese prefers to frame outcomes as things that became, rather than things someone did. 「わかるようになりました」 "it came to be that I understand" (= I've learned to). You'll meet ようになる・ことになる in JP-22 — they're the same becoming-verb scaled up to whole clauses. Install なる now and that chapter will feel free.

Before and after: 前に・後で

寝ねる前まえに、単語たんごを復習ふくしゅうします。Before sleeping, I review vocabulary. (dictionary form + 前に)
仕事しごとが終おわった後あとで、ジムに行いきます。After work ends, I go to the gym. (た-form + 後で)

Note the tense logic: 前に always takes dictionary form (the act hasn't happened yet from the clause's viewpoint), 後で always takes た-form (it has). Japanese subclause tense is relative, not absolute — a theme that will matter a lot in reading.

Vocabulary — weather, seasons & nature

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習

JP-15

Relative clauses — sentences that dress nouns

Japanese has no "who/which/that". A whole sentence simply parks in front of a noun. This is the point where reading real Japanese either clicks or collapses — make it click.

The mechanism

Put a plain-form sentence directly before a noun, and it modifies that noun. No pronoun, no comma, no glue:

[昨日きのう買かった]本ほんを読よんでいます。I'm reading the book [I bought yesterday].
[眼鏡めがねをかけている]人ひとは誰だれですか。Who is the person [wearing glasses]?
[肉にくを食たべない]人ひとも多おおいです。There are also many people [who don't eat meat].

Inside the bracket, everything is plain form (even in a polite sentence), and the subject of the clause takes が or の, never は: [友達ともだちが作つくった]ケーキ "the cake my friend made". は marks main-sentence topics only — this is the sharpest は/が rule there is, and it's mechanical.

How to read long sentences without drowning

Japanese noun phrases grow leftward. The survival algorithm, which you should practice consciously for the next month:

  1. Find the particle after a noun chunk (は・が・を・に…). Everything before it up to the previous particle-boundary is one box.
  2. If a box seems too long, find its final noun — that's the real cargo; everything before it inside the box is decoration.
  3. The main verb is at the very end. Read the boxes, then the verb, then re-read once. Two passes is normal at this stage; one pass comes with volume.
[去年きょねん京都きょうとで会あった友達ともだち]が[先週せんしゅう送おくってくれた写真しゃしん]を見みました。I looked at the photos [that the friend [whom I met in Kyoto last year] sent me last week]. — two nested boxes, one verb.

こと and の — turning verbs into nouns

To use a whole action as a noun ("swimming is fun"), nominalize with の or こと:

泳およぐのが好すきです。I like swimming.
私わたしの趣味しゅみは写真しゃしんを撮とることです。My hobby is taking photos. (こと before です)

Rough division: の for direct perception and likes (見るのが・聞くのが・〜のが好き/上手), こと for abstract statements and before です. When in doubt in speech, の.

Vocabulary — body & health

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — parse the brackets

JP-16

Conditionals — と・ば・たら・なら

Four ways to say "if", each with its own logic. Learners agonize over choosing; the secret is that たら covers 80% and the other three have one sharp job each.

The four, by their sharp job

FormCore logicSignature example
とNatural/automatic consequence — "whenever X, Y follows"春になると、桜さくらが咲さきます。
When spring comes, cherries bloom.
ばPure hypothesis — "if and only if X held"安やすければ、買かいます。
If it's cheap, I'll buy it.
たらAfter/if X happens (one-time, sequential) — the general-purpose "if/when"家いえに着ついたら、電話でんわしてください。
When you get home, call me.
ならGiven that topic — "if we're talking about X"ラーメンなら、あの店みせがいい。
If it's ramen you want, that shop's the one.

Formation: と takes plain non-past + と. ば: Group 1 え-row + ば (行けば), Group 2 れば (食べれば), い-adj くければ→ければ (安ければ), noun/な-adj なら(ば). たら: た-form + ら (行ったら・安かったら・雨だったら). なら: plain form or noun + なら.

The two rules that prevent 90% of errors

Rule 1: と forbids willful results

After と, the result must be automatic — no commands, invitations, or decisions. ×春になると、花見をしましょう. Want to follow "if" with "let's/please/I will"? Use たら. ○春になったら、花見をしましょう.

Rule 2: なら reverses time

なら is the only conditional where the main clause can happen before the if-clause: 日本に行くなら、JRパスを買ってください "If you're going to Japan, buy a JR Pass (before going)". たら would mean buy it after arriving. なら reacts to information ("given what you just said…"); たら reacts to events.

Reading-comprehension bonus: と in narrative

In written Japanese, 〜と、 often means "and then (I discovered)": ドアを開あけると、猫ねこがいた "When I opened the door, there was a cat." Fiction runs on this と — recognize it and short stories become readable.

Vocabulary — travel & transport

Genki word list — the aligned lesson’s own vocabulary

練習 — pick the conditional

N4-01

N4 readiness checkpoint

You've passed through N5. This is the honest checkpoint before the N3 climb: what N4 actually adds, a mock that samples it, and a frank note on what this book gives you here versus what you must add.

What N4 is, concretely

N4 roughly doubles N5: about 300 kanji (up from ~106), 1,500 words (up from ~800), and the grammar in chapters JP-11 through JP-21 — potential, giving/receiving, conditionals, passive, causative, and the evidential system (そう・よう・らしい). If you can read the JP-11–21 example sentences without the furigana and pass the mock below, you are genuinely at the N4 door.

Honest scope note

This book teaches all N4 grammar and a strong slice of N4 vocabulary, but it does not yet contain a dedicated N4 kanji program (the ~190 kanji beyond N5) or an N4-specific listening/reading gym at the depth the N5 gate has. For those, pair this checkpoint with Genki II (the chip on each chapter shows the lesson) and an N4 kanji deck. The coverage map on the Road to N5 chapter tracks your grammar completeness across N5, N4, and N3 so you can see exactly what's left.

The N4 mock

Twenty-five questions sampled from everything in Parts II–IV — vocabulary, the harder grammar, and the N3-bridge reading. Same rules as the N5 mock: no blanks, read the question first, 60% is the working bar.

Reading at N4 speed

The timed reading gym below pulls short unseen texts and clocks you. N4 reading is mostly N5 reading at pace — the goal is under a minute per short text, with comprehension intact.

Where to go from here

If the N4 mock holds above 70% twice: start Part V (N3 Consolidation) as your grammar track, but shift your daily weight to volume — the Dojo in recall mode, the reading gym, and real listening. N3 is not won by learning more patterns; it's won by meeting the ones you know ten thousand more times. The book is your map; from here the territory is immersion.

JP-17

Passive & causative — れる・られる・せる・させる

Two conjugations that reorganize who does what to whom — and one of them carries an emotional payload English passives don't have.

The passive — れる/られる

Group 1: あ-row + れる (書く→書かれる・読む→読まれる). Group 2: られる (食べられる — yes, identical to the potential; context disambiguates). する→される, 来る→来られる. The doer takes に:

この本ほんは多おおくの人ひとに読よまれています。This book is read by many people. (neutral passive)

The suffering passive

Japanese also passivizes intransitive verbs to mean "this happened AND it hurt me" — a structure with no English equivalent:

雨あめに降ふられました。I got rained on (and it ruined things).
隣となりの人ひとにタバコを吸すわれました。The person next to me smoked (at my expense).
Mental model

The Japanese passive's core meaning is "an event landed on me from outside". Sometimes that's neutral description (books being read), often it's grievance. When you read 〜られた and the subject seems unaffected-yet-mentioned, look for the victim reading — it changes the emotional color of the whole passage.

The causative — せる/させる

Group 1: あ-row + せる (書かせる), Group 2: させる (食べさせる), する→させる, 来る→来させる. Meaning splits on the causee's will:

部長ぶちょうは私わたしに報告書ほうこくしょを書かかせました。The department head made me write the report. (coercion)
子供こどもを公園こうえんで遊あそばせました。I let the kids play in the park. (permission)

The polite request 〜させてください "please let me…" is the causative's most useful form: 考かんがえさせてください "let me think about it" — essential business Japanese.

Causative-passive: made to do

飲のみ会かいで歌うたを歌うたわされました。I was made to sing at the drinking party. (させられる, often contracted される→さされる… feel the resentment)

Vocabulary — society & incidents

練習

JP-18

Seeming & hearsay — そう・よう・らしい・みたい

Japanese grammatically distinguishes what you saw, what you infer, and what you were told. English lets "seems" blur it all; Japanese won't — and the JLPT tests exactly this.

The evidence system

FormEvidence typeAttachExample
〜そう (appearance)Direct visual impression, about to happenverb stem / adj stem雨あめが降ふりそうです (looks like rain)・おいしそう (looks tasty)
〜そう (hearsay)I heard/read that…plain form + そうだ降ふるそうです (I hear it'll rain)
〜ようだ／みたいMy inference from evidenceplain + ようだ (みたい casual)だれもいないようです (it appears no one's here)
〜らしいSecondhand + it fits the typeplain + らしいあの店みせは有名ゆうめいらしい (apparently famous)
The そう collision

Same word, two grammars, opposite meanings. おいしそう (stem attach) = "looks tasty [I'm looking at it]". おいしいそう (plain-form attach) = "I hear it's tasty [never seen it]". One kana of difference — 降りそう vs 降るそう — flips eyewitness to rumor. The JLPT loves this; so do misunderstandings.

Bonus uses: Xらしい after a noun means "true to X's nature" (男おとこらしい "manly", 春はるらしい天気てんき "properly spring-like weather"). Xのようだ／Xみたい after a noun means "like X" (夢ゆめのようだ "like a dream").

〜がる — displaying feelings

The third-person rule from JP-11 completes here: adjective stem + がる = "shows signs of". 恥はずかしがる "act embarrassed", 怖こわがっている "is (visibly) scared". Emotion adjectives about third parties want がる, ようだ, or らしい — direct statement is reserved for your own chest.

Vocabulary — communication & judgment

練習 — whose evidence?

JP-19

Keigo — the politeness machine

Not "extra polite words" but a system: raise the other side, lower your side, polish the surface. Learn the dozen special verbs and the two templates, and service-counter Japan opens up.

Three modes

丁寧語 — polite style

です・ます. You've been using it since JP-02. Direction-neutral polish.

尊敬語 — honorific

Raises the other person's actions. Template: お + stem + になる (お読よみになる), or special verbs.

謙譲語 — humble

Lowers your own actions toward them. Template: お + stem + する (お持もちします "I'll carry it"), or special verbs.

The special verbs — the real vocabulary of keigo

PlainHonorific (their act)Humble (your act)
行く・来るいらっしゃる参まいる・伺うかがう
いるいらっしゃるおる
食べる・飲む召めし上あがるいただく
言うおっしゃる申もうす・申し上げる
見るご覧らんになる拝見はいけんする
するなさるいたす
知っているご存ぞんじだ存ぞんじている
社長しゃちょうはもう帰かえられました／お帰りになりました。The president has already left. (honorific — れる-passive doubles as light keigo)
明日あした、伺うかがいます。I will visit (humbly) tomorrow.

Survival keigo — what you'll actually hear

Konbini and restaurant staff speak a ritualized keigo. Decode kit: いらっしゃいませ (welcome), 〜でございます (super-polite です), お待またせいたしました (sorry to keep you waiting), 少々しょうしょうお待ちください (one moment please), 〜てもよろしいでしょうか (may I…). You need to produce very little of this — but recognizing it instantly is an N3 listening requirement and a daily-life one.

Strategy

Learn keigo as vocabulary, not conjugation: いらっしゃる、いただく、おっしゃる are just words with a [+respect-up] or [+humble-down] tag. Ten special verbs + the お〜になる/お〜する templates cover 95% of what N3–N2 asks. The remaining 5% is a lifetime of Japanese corporate emails, which is not this book's problem.

Vocabulary — office & service situations

練習 — raise or lower?

JP-20

Pairs — transitive & intransitive verbs

開く and 開ける. 出る and 出す. Japanese verbs come in door-opens/I-open-door couples, and choosing the wrong twin is the most persistent intermediate error there is.

The concept

自動詞 — intransitive

The event happens by itself; subject takes が. ドアが開あく "the door opens."

他動詞 — transitive

An agent does it to something; object takes を. ドアを開あける "(I) open the door."

English recycles one verb ("the door opens / I open the door"); Japanese gives each role its own verb. There are recurring sound patterns (〜aru intransitive / 〜eru transitive: 閉まる/閉める・止まる/止める; 〜eru intransitive / 〜su transitive: 出る/出す・消える/消す) but no full rule — the pairs are vocabulary, and this chapter's table IS the vocabulary set.

Why it matters: ている vs てある, and blame

ドアが開あいている。The door is open. (state, nobody blamed — intransitive + ている)
ドアが開あけてある。The door has been (deliberately) left open. (transitive + てある — someone did this on purpose)
エアコンをつけておきます。I'll turn on the AC in advance. (〜ておく = do ahead/leave in place for later)

The triad: 自動詞+ている = neutral state; 他動詞+てある = state with intent behind it; 他動詞+ておく = preparation. Japanese speakers encode agency and intention here without thinking; when you say ×お金が落とした instead of お金を落とした, natives hear grammar pain.

The classic excuse pair

壊こわれた "it broke (on its own)" vs 壊した "I broke it". Choosing the intransitive is the built-in way to dodge responsibility — 「コップが割れちゃった」 "the cup went and broke itself". Understand this and you understand a great deal of Japanese office communication.

The pair table & matcher

Twenty core pairs, drilled both directions. This is pure memorization with pattern support — the matcher below never gets old at parties. (Lie: it does. But it works.)

Vocabulary — the pairs as words

JP-21

Timing & aspect — ところ・ばかり・ながら・うちに

Where English says "just", "while", "about to", Japanese has a precision toolkit for locating an action on its own timeline. N3 reading leans on these constantly.

ところ — the freeze-frame

ところ ("place") after a verb pinpoints where on the action's timeline you are — the tense of the verb before it does the aiming:

食べるところabout to eat (dictionary = not yet started)
食べているところin the middle of eating
食べたところjust finished eating (fresh)
今、家いえを出でるところです。I'm just about to leave the house. (the classic phone reply)

ばかり — "just did" and "nothing but"

日本にほんに来きたばかりです。I just came to Japan (recently — subjective freshness, unlike たところ's literal seconds-ago).
弟おとうとはゲームばかりしています。My little brother does nothing but game. (noun + ばかり = "only ever")

ながら — two actions, one body

音楽おんがくを聞ききながら、勉強べんきょうします。I study while listening to music. (stem + ながら; main action comes second)

うちに & 間（あいだ）— windows of time

熱あついうちに食たべてください。Eat it while it's hot. (うちに = before the window closes — urgency built in)
夏休なつやすみの間あいだ、実家じっかにいました。During summer break, I was at my parents'. (間 = throughout the span; 間に = at some point within it)

てしまう — completion and regret

全部ぜんぶ食たべてしまいました。I ate it all (completely / oops). Contractions: 〜ちゃう/〜じゃう — 食べちゃった.

Two flavors, disambiguated by context: clean completion ("read the whole thing") and irreversible mistake ("went and deleted it"). The contraction ちゃった is overwhelmingly the regret flavor in speech.

ていく・てくる — change with a direction

だんだん寒さむくなってきました。It's been getting colder (change arriving toward now).
これから暖あたたかくなっていくでしょう。It will keep getting warmer from here (change heading away from now).

てくる = change flowing toward the present; ていく = change flowing onward from it. The camera from JP-13, applied to time. Once you see it, half of N3 reading passages' final paragraphs (which love 〜ていくだろう) become transparent.

Vocabulary — time & change

練習

JP-22

N3 battery I — purpose, extent, becoming

The first sweep of true N3 patterns: doing things so that, to the extent that, deciding and becoming. Each is small; together they're the connective tissue of adult Japanese.

Purpose: ために vs ように

〜ために

Purposeful action by a willful agent — "in order to".

家いえを買かうために、貯金ちょきんしています。
I'm saving in order to buy a house.

〜ように

Aimed-for state, often outside direct control — "so that". Takes potential/negative/stative verbs.

忘わすれないように、メモします。
I take notes so that I won't forget.

Rule of thumb: if the verb before it is something you decide to do → ために. If it's a state you hope results (できる, わかる, 忘れない…) → ように. ために after a noun is "for the sake of": 家族かぞくのために.

ようにする・ようになる・ことにする・ことになる

The four-cell grid every N3 exam probes. する = someone decides; なる = the world shifts:

〜ようにする／なる (gradual state)〜ことにする／なる (decision/arrangement)
する (I act)毎日歩くようにしています
I make a point of walking daily		会社を辞めることにしました
I decided to quit the company
なる (it came to be)漢字が読めるようになりました
I've become able to read kanji		大阪に転勤することになりました
it's been arranged that I transfer to Osaka
ことになりました — the institutional passive

Japanese announces life events as arrangements that happened to one: marriages, transfers, resignations all arrive as 〜ことになりました even when the speaker chose them. It's modesty grammar — you'll meet it in every formal announcement email ever written.

Extent: ば〜ほど, ほど, くらい

漢字かんじは勉強べんきょうすればするほど面白おもしろくなります。The more you study kanji, the more interesting they get. (ば-form + repeat + ほど)
歩あるけないほど疲つかれました。I'm tired to the point of not being able to walk. (ほど = upper-bound extent)
泣なきたいくらい嬉うれしかった。I was happy enough to cry. (くらい = approximate extent, lighter than ほど)

Noun + ほど with negative = "not as … as": 今年ことしは去年きょねんほど寒さむくない "this year isn't as cold as last."

Viewpoint markers: にとって・について・に対して

Xにとってfrom X's standpoint私わたしにとって、家族かぞくが一番いちばん大切たいせつです。
Xについてabout/concerning X日本にほんの歴史れきしについて調しらべました。
Xに対してtoward X (attitude/contrast)お客きゃくさんに対して失礼しつれいなことを言いってはいけない。

Vocabulary — abstract nouns I

練習 — the battery, live-fire

JP-23

N3 battery II — expectation, obligation, reason

はず, べき, わけ — three flavors of "should" that English merges and Japanese splits by whether the source is logic, morality, or explanation.

The three "should"s

〜はずだ

Logical expectation. "Given what I know, it must be so."

荷物にもつは今日きょう届とどくはずです。
The package should arrive today (tracking says so).

はずがない = logically impossible.

〜べきだ

Moral/normative should. "The right thing is to…" Strong; careful aiming it at superiors.

約束やくそくは守まもるべきです。
Promises should be kept.

するべき→すべき is the tidy classical form.

〜わけだ

Explanatory conclusion. "So THAT's why / naturally it follows."

十年じゅうねん住すんでいたんですか。上手じょうずなわけですね。
You lived there ten years? No wonder you're fluent.

The わけ family — negation's minefield

わけではない"it's not that…" (partial denial)嫌きらいなわけではないけど… it's not that I dislike it, but…
わけがない"there's no way that…" (flat impossibility)そんなに簡単かんたんなわけがない。No way it's that easy.
わけにはいかない"can't very well…" (socially/morally blocked)今日きょうは休やすむわけにはいかない。I can't exactly take today off.

Cause and credit: おかげで・せいで・ため（に）

先生せんせいのおかげで、合格ごうかくしました。Thanks to my teacher, I passed. (credit)
雪ゆきのせいで、電車でんしゃが止とまりました。Because of the snow, trains stopped. (blame)

ため(に) is the neutral, written-register cause: 事故じこのため、遅おくれています — the version you'll hear in station announcements. Same word as purpose-ために; tense and context split them (past/noun-の + ため = cause).

Probability dial: かもしれない → でしょう → に違いない

Calibrate your certainty: かもしれない "might" (~30%) → でしょう／だろう "probably" (~70%) → に違ちがいない "must be" (~95%, technically N2 but it belongs in this set). All attach to plain form. Weather reports live at でしょう; detectives live at に違いない.

Vocabulary — opinion & reasoning

練習

JP-24

Reading gym I — three N3 passages

Everything so far, integrated. Read each passage twice — once peeking at nothing, once checking readings — then take the comprehension quiz. This is what the JLPT actually feels like.

How to train here

  1. First pass, no furigana: read for gist. Mark (mentally) where you stumbled.
  2. Second pass: hover the stumbles. Say each repaired sentence aloud once.
