  3. Quiz. Then — the step everyone skips — read it one final time at full speed. That last pass is where reading speed is actually built.

読み物 1 — 自己紹介

はじめまして。カルロスと申もうします。ブラジルから来きました。日本にほんの会社かいしゃで働はたらく前まえは、サンパウロの大学だいがくでコンピューターを勉強べんきょうしていました。日本語にほんごは二年間にねんかん勉強べんきょうしていますが、漢字かんじを読よむのはまだ難むずかしいです。でも、毎日まいにち少すこしずつ読よめるようになってきました。週末しゅうまつは同僚どうりょうとフットサルをしたり、日本にほんの映画えいがを見みたりします。いつか字幕じまくなしで映画えいがが分わかるようになりたいと思おもっています。Nice to meet you. My name is Carlos, from Brazil. Before working at a Japanese company, I studied computing at a university in São Paulo. I've studied Japanese for two years, but reading kanji is still hard. Still, little by little I've become able to read. On weekends I play futsal with coworkers and watch Japanese films. Someday I want to understand films without subtitles.

読み物 2 — お知らせ

マンションにお住すまいの皆様みなさまへ。来週らいしゅうの火曜日かようび、午前ごぜん九時くじから十二時じゅうにじまで、水道すいどうの工事こうじのため、水みずが使つかえなくなります。ご不便ふべんをおかけしますが、ご協力きょうりょくをお願ねがいいたします。なお、工事こうじが早はやく終おわった場合ばあいは、その時点じてんで水みずをお使つかいいただけます。質問しつもんがある方かたは、管理人かんりにんまでご連絡れんらくください。To all building residents: next Tuesday from 9:00 to 12:00, due to water-line construction, water will be unavailable. We apologize for the inconvenience and ask for your cooperation. If work finishes early, water will be usable from that point. Questions: contact the building manager.

Notice the register: お住まいの皆様, ご不便をおかけします, お使いいただけます — this is JP-19's keigo doing its most common real-world job: notices. Every announcement in Japan is written exactly like this one.

読み物 3 — 意見文

最近さいきん、紙かみの本ほんより電子書籍でんししょせきを読よむ人ひとが増ふえているそうだ。電子書籍でんししょせきは場所ばしょを取とらないし、何冊なんさつも持もち歩あるけるので便利べんりだ。しかし、私わたしは紙かみの本ほんのほうが好すきだ。読よんだページの厚あつさで、どこまで進すすんだかが手てに分わかるからだ。技術ぎじゅつが進すすめば進すすむほど、逆ぎゃくに紙かみの良よさに気きづく人ひとも増ふえていくのではないだろうか。Apparently more people now read e-books than paper books. E-books take no space and you can carry many, so they're convenient. But I prefer paper: the thickness of the pages you've read tells your hand how far you've come. The more technology advances, might not the number of people who notice paper's virtues grow too?

This is the N3 essay voice: plain だ-style, そうだ hearsay, し-listing, ば〜ほど, and the soft rhetorical close のではないだろうか. You will meet this exact voice on every JLPT reading section from N3 up.

Comprehension check

Vocabulary — from the passages

JP-25

Written Japanese — である and the news register

Newspapers, reports, and Wikipedia are written in a register you've barely met. It's not harder — it's a small set of substitutions. Learn the mapping and formal text unlocks overnight.

The である style

Formal writing replaces です/だ with である (negative: ではない, past: であった/だった). It's not politer — it's objective, the voice of essays, theses, and encyclopedias:

富士山ふじさんは日本にほんで最もっとも高たかい山やまである。Mt. Fuji is the highest mountain in Japan. (encyclopedia voice; note 最も for 一番)

Whole-register substitution table — reading fluency here is mostly running these mappings automatically:

Spoken/neutralWritten/formalMeaning
でも・だけどしかし・だがbut
だからしたがって・そのためtherefore
〜と一緒いっしょに〜とともにtogether with
AとBA及およびBA and B
AかBA又またはBA or B
〜について〜に関かんしてconcerning
いま現在げんざいnow / at present

Headline grammar — the compressed dialect

Headlines drop particles, drop です/する, and end on nouns (体言止たいげんどめ):

首相しゅしょう、来月らいげつ訪米ほうべいへ"PM to visit US next month" — the comma replaces は, へ means "heading toward (planned)", no verb at all.

Decode kit for the five most compressed headline signals: へ (planned), か (possibly/suspected), も (even/also, hinting surprise), で (cause: 地震で = because of the quake), verbless noun endings (発表 = "announces"). Reading five headlines a day with this kit is the single cheapest N2 reading habit.

漢語 density — why formal text feels harder

Written Japanese swaps native verbs for kanji-compound + する verbs: 調べる→調査ちょうさする, 使う→使用しようする, 始まる→開始かいしする. The meaning is identical; the texture is denser. Your kanji knowledge is the cheat code here — 調査 is transparent if you know 調 and 査. This chapter's vocab set is exactly these paired swaps, and after it, newspaper sentences stop feeling like walls.

Vocabulary — news & formal pairs

練習 — translate the register

JP-26

N2 battery I — change, contrast, correlation

The patterns that let a text say "as X progressed, Y…; on the one hand…; compared with…" — the skeleton of every analytical paragraph you'll read from now on.

Change in tandem: につれて・に伴って・とともに

年としをとるにつれて、時間じかんが早はやく感かんじられるようになる。As you age, time comes to feel faster. (X changes → Y drifts with it)
経済けいざいの発展はってんに伴ともなって、環境かんきょう問題もんだいも増ふえた。With economic development, environmental problems also grew. (formal, accompaniment)

All three take a change-word before them and a change-clause after. につれて is gradual drift, に伴って is formal accompaniment, とともに doubles as plain "together with". If both clauses don't involve change, none of these fit — that's the exam trap.

Contrast: 一方（で）・反面・のに対して

都市としの人口じんこうは増ふえる一方いっぽうで、地方ちほうは減へり続つづけている。While city populations grow, rural ones keep shrinking.
この仕事しごとは楽たのしい反面はんめん、責任せきにんも重おもい。This job is fun; on the flip side, the responsibility is heavy. (two faces of one thing)

Related but different: 〜一方いっぽうだ sentence-finally means "keeps moving in one direction only": 物価ぶっかは上あがる一方だ "prices just keep rising."

Counter-expectation: わりに・くせに・ものの

〜わりに(は)considering X, surprisingly Yこの店みせは値段ねだんのわりにおいしい。good for the price
〜くせにeven though (accusatory — aims at people)知しっているくせに、教おしえてくれない。you know, yet you won't tell me
〜もののalthough (written; expectation unfulfilled)免許めんきょを取とったものの、運転うんてんする機会きかいがない。got a license, but no chance to drive

Scope additions: ばかりでなく・はもちろん・に限かぎらず

この薬くすりは大人おとなばかりでなく子供こどもにも使つかえる。This medicine can be used not only by adults but also by children. (…も follows in the second half)

All three mean "not just X" — ばかりでなく neutral, はもちろん "X goes without saying", に限らず "not limited to X" (formal). Second clause almost always carries a も.

Vocabulary — society & economy

練習

JP-27

N2 battery II — judgment, limits, inevitability

"Can't help but", "liable to", "nothing but" — the grammar of pressure and limits. These patterns are rare in speech and everywhere in editorials, which is exactly why they gatekeep N2.

Inevitability & risk

〜ざるを得えないhave no choice but to (formal; ない-stem + ざる; する→せざる)計画けいかくを変更へんこうせざるを得ない。we're forced to change the plan
〜かねないcould well (happen) — bad outcomes only事故じこになりかねない。this could well end in an accident
〜かねるcannot (politely decline ability)お答こたえしかねます。we are unable to answer
〜おそれがあるthere is a risk of (news voice)台風たいふうが上陸じょうりくするおそれがある。
かねない vs かねる

Mirror twins: かねない = "quite possibly WILL (and it's bad)"; かねる = "can NOT (politely)". The negative-looking one is the affirmative threat. 承知しかねます in a business mail means "no". Exam setters adore this pair; so do insurance documents.

The もの family

〜ものだgeneral truth / nostalgia / mild admonition人ひとは失敗しっぱいから学まなぶものだ。people (by nature) learn from failure
〜というものだthat's precisely what X is / that's just…それはわがままというものだ。that's what we call selfishness
〜ものだからbecause (excuse-flavored)道みちが混こんでいたものですから…the roads were jammed, you see…

Simultaneity with tension: つつ・つつある

悪わるいと知しりつつ、夜中よなかにラーメンを食たべた。Knowing it was bad, I still ate midnight ramen. (つつ = while/though, written register)
状況じょうきょうは改善かいぜんしつつある。The situation is (in the process of) improving. (つつある = ongoing directional change, news voice)

Timing triggers: 次第・たとたん・か〜ないかのうちに

詳細しょうさいが分わかり次第しだい、ご連絡れんらくします。As soon as details are known, we'll contact you. (stem + 次第 — business standard)
ドアを開あけたとたん、猫ねこが飛とび出だした。The instant I opened the door, the cat shot out. (た + とたん — surprise required)

Vocabulary — argument & policy

練習

JP-28

Nuance — onomatopoeia, collocations, word choice

The layer that separates textbook Japanese from real Japanese: sound-words with grammar of their own, noun-verb marriages you can't divorce, and the three vocabulary strata.

Onomatopoeia is a grammar, not a garnish

Japanese has thousands of sound/state words (擬音語 sound-mimicking, 擬態語 state-mimicking), and they follow rules: reduplication (ドキドキ), 〜っと forms (さっと), and they usually hook into sentences with と or する:

ドキドキするheart pounding (nerves/excitement)ワクワクするthrilled with anticipation
イライラするirritatedぼんやりするspacing out
ぐっすり寝ねるsleep soundlyぺらぺら話はなすspeak fluently
しとしと降ふるrain drizzlingざあざあ降ふるrain pouring
The sound-symbolism cheat

Voiced consonants (だ・ざ・ば行) = heavy/rough/big; unvoiced = light/small. さらさら (smooth-flowing) vs ざらざら (gritty). ころころ (small thing rolling) vs ごろごろ (boulder rolling; also you, lazing on Sunday). Guess unknown onomatopoeia with this rule and you'll be right disturbingly often.

Collocations — the 気 economy and friends

Japanese runs on noun+verb set phrases where the verb is not guessable. The 気 family alone is daily-conversation currency:

気きがつくnotice気をつけるbe careful
気にするworry about (dwell)気になるbe on one's mind (bug you)
気が合あうget along気が進すすまないnot feel like it

Same story with 〜を取とる・〜がかかる・〜を引ひく: 電話をかける, 風邪を引く, 時間がかかる, メモを取る. Learn the marriage, never just the noun — every vocab table from here on gives collocation examples in the drill.

The three strata — 和語・漢語・外来語

Most concepts exist in three flavors: native (宿やど), Sino-Japanese (旅館りょかん), Western loan (ホテル) — with rising formality/technicality from 和語 to 漢語, and 外来語 marking modernity. Same with 取り消す／キャンセル／解約. Choosing the stratum IS the style: emails to clients run 漢語-heavy, chats with friends run 和語-heavy. When two words "mean the same thing", stratum is usually the difference.

Vocabulary — onomatopoeia & set phrases

練習

JP-29

Reading gym II — two N2 passages

Longer, denser, opinionated — the register of the actual N2 exam and of every column you'll read after it. Same three-pass protocol as JP-24; expect the first pass to hurt a little. That's the workout.

読み物 1 — 「便利」の代償だいしょう

スマートフォンの普及ふきゅうに伴ともない、私わたしたちの生活せいかつは格段かくだんに便利べんりになった。地図ちずを読よむ必要ひつようもなければ、電話番号でんわばんごうを覚おぼえる必要ひつようもない。しかし、便利べんりさと引ひき換かえに、私わたしたちは何なにかを失うしないつつあるのではないか。例たとえば、道みちに迷まよう経験けいけんである。迷まよった末すえに偶然ぐうぜん見みつけた小ちいさな店みせ、知しらない人ひとに道みちを尋たずねる気きまずさと温あたたかさ。そうした偶然ぐうぜんは、効率こうりつを追求ついきゅうするあまり、切きり捨すてられてしまった。技術ぎじゅつを否定ひていするつもりはない。ただ、便利べんりさを選えらぶたびに何なにを手放てばなしているのか、時々ときどき立たち止どまって考かんがえる価値かちはあるのではないだろうか。With the spread of smartphones, life has become dramatically convenient — no need to read maps or memorize phone numbers. But in exchange, are we not gradually losing something? For example, the experience of getting lost: the tiny shop found by chance after wandering, the awkward warmth of asking a stranger for directions. In our pursuit of efficiency, such chance encounters have been discarded. I don't mean to reject technology. But each time we choose convenience, it may be worth pausing to ask what we're letting go of.

読み物 2 — 社内しゃないメール

各位かくい　お疲つかれ様さまです。総務部そうむぶの山田やまだです。さて、本社ほんしゃビルの電気設備でんきせつび点検てんけんのため、下記かきの通とおり停電ていでんを実施じっしいたします。作業中さぎょうちゅうはサーバーにアクセスできかねますので、必要ひつようなデータは前日ぜんじつまでに各自かくじダウンロードしておいてください。なお、点検てんけんが終了しゅうりょうし次第しだい、改あらためてご連絡れんらくいたします。ご不明ふめいな点てんがございましたら、山田やまだまでお問とい合あわせください。よろしくお願ねがいいたします。To all: This is Yamada from General Affairs. Due to electrical inspection of the head office building, a power outage will be carried out as below. During the work the servers will be inaccessible, so please download any needed data by the previous day. We will contact you again as soon as the inspection is complete. For questions, contact Yamada. Thank you.

This is the full corporate stack in one mail: 各位, さて as topic-shifter, いたします humble forms, かねます as the polite "can't", 次第, ございましたら. Nothing here is decoration — every phrase is load-bearing convention, and N2 listening/reading assumes you parse it at a glance.

Comprehension check

Vocabulary — from the passages

JP-30

The road from here — immersion, SRS, and the exam

This book took you from zero to the N2 doorway. What carries you through it is volume — here is exactly how to structure that volume, and how to keep this book working for you.

The honest map

Grammar-wise, you now hold everything N3 tests and the core of N2. What remains is not new machinery — it's mileage: thousands more words met in context, hundreds of hours of listening, and reading speed. The gap between "knows the grammar" and "passes N2" is almost entirely reps, which is good news: reps are schedulable.

The weekly template (fits around a job)

Daily (20–40 min)Dojo drill → one chapter (new or review) → that chapter's drills. Weekdays are maintenance; never zero.
Weekend block 1Reading volume: NHK News Web Easy → graduate to regular NHK articles using the JP-25 headline kit. Tadoku graded readers fill the fiction gap.
Weekend block 2Listening: one episode of anything Japanese you already like, Japanese subtitles on. Rewatch a scene until the subtitles feel slow.
MonthlyOne full JLPT practice section (free official samples exist), timed. The exam is a format skill as well as a language skill.

SRS: your vocabulary flywheel

This book gave you ~1,200 words with readings — the core. To reach N2's ~6,000, add a spaced-repetition system (Anki with a core N3/N2 deck, or WaniKani if you want kanji-reading reinforcement structured for you). Rules that keep SRS from eating your life: 20 new cards/day maximum, reviews before new cards, and every word you miss twice in the Dojo goes into your deck manually — self-harvested cards stick better than any pre-made deck.

Companion resources (the short list)

  • Tofugu — Learn Japanese roadmap — the philosophy this book's onboarding borrows: know ~80% of anything before you study it, and front-load kanji+vocab so grammar never hurts.
  • WaniKani — kanji + vocabulary SRS with radical mnemonics; the strongest complement to this book if you want your kanji readings drilled on autopilot.
  • Genki I & II — every chapter up to JP-21 carries a Genki chip; use Genki's dialogues and workbook as your written-practice layer for the same grammar.
  • NHK News Web Easy — furigana news; your first "real Japanese" from Part IV onward.
  • Tadoku graded readers — free leveled fiction for reading volume.

The exam itself

  • N3 first, as a checkpoint — it's cheap calibration for the N2 attempt, and passing it funds motivation for the long N2 grind.
  • 読解 strategy: read the question before the passage; the JLPT hides answers behind paraphrase, so match meaning, not words. Your JP-22/23/26/27 batteries are precisely the paraphrase inventory.
  • 聴解 strategy: answers hinge on reversals — けど、やっぱり、ということで. Train your ear to perk up at contrast markers; the correct answer usually lives after the last one.
  • Timing: reading is the section people fail on time, not knowledge. Your daily three-pass gym habit is the fix — keep it.

Keep the book alive

The Dojo never expires: with all 31 chapters done, it drills the full 1,200-word pool — five minutes of maintenance a day. Reset any chapter's "done" state to pull it out of rotation for focused review. Re-read the grammar batteries (JP-22, 23, 26, 27) monthly; they compress at each pass. And when you pass through the gate — 合格ごうかく — the book's last service is reference: the tables don't age.

The final drill

Everything, all chapters, no mercy. This is the same instrument as the Dojo, but it always uses the complete vocabulary pool — your graduation exam, repeatable forever:

継続けいぞくは力ちからなり。— 門もんはいつも開あいている。

Persistence is power. The gate is always open.

The Gate — a self-contained interactive Japanese course. All content, styling, audio (browser speech synthesis), and instruments are inline; nothing is fetched from the network.
